text: All common age-predicted maximal heart rate equations (Fox, Gellish, Gulati, Tanaka, Arena, Astrand, Nes, Fairbarn) show poor agreement with measured HRmax based on wide Bland-Altman limits of agreement.

The Fox equation (220 - age) may be the best age-predicted HRmax option for general populations because it shows less proportional bias — it is less likely to systematically under- or overestimate based on individual HRmax.
Most age-predicted HRmax equations exhibit proportional bias: they underestimate HRmax in individuals with lower true HRmax and overestimate in individuals with higher true HRmax.
The Gulati, Astrand, Nes, and Fairbarn (male) equations produce statistically significant differences from measured HRmax in a general population (p < 0.05).
Graded exercise testing (GXT) to volitional fatigue with RER > 1.10 should be used to determine true HRmax when accuracy is needed, as age-prediction equations are unreliable at the individual level.

Training zones based on age-predicted HRmax may be inaccurate for individuals whose true HRmax deviates substantially from the population mean, making field-based or lab-based HRmax determination preferable for precise training prescription.
